stories.title: 'They've come so far: Kenosha woman who rescued 3 abandoned dogs says they're ready for their forever homes
stories.summary: Three dogs thrown from a moving vehicle in Milwaukee have fully recovered thanks to rescuer Kaley Anne and Billy's Posse. They're now healthy, playful, and ready for adoption. The community has rallied around them, making them local celebrities.
stories.extended_summary: Magnolia, Brownie, and Case were thrown from a moving car and left to die, with severe injuries and Tylenol poisoning. Case's leg wound nearly hit a major artery. Rescuer Kaley Anne nursed them back to health over weeks. Now fully recovered, they're eager for forever homes. 'They just want someone to open their hearts,' Kaley says. The community response has been overwhelming, and the dogs have become local stars.
stories.display_reason: hero always uses image mode (summary always visible)

stories.title: 44-acre solar farm to be built in Wrangell
stories.summary: A 44-acre solar farm is coming to Wrangell, Alaska, aiming to replace expensive diesel power during maintenance outages. The project starts at 1.5 megawatts and promises to slash costs and boost energy resilience for the island community.
stories.extended_summary: Wrangell burns diesel for at least a week each June, costing around $250,000. A new solar farm will offset that entirely. Borough manager Mason Villarma calls it 'huge for economic development.' The farm will start at 1.5 MW and expand to 5 MW with battery storage, keeping lights on during outages. It complements the town's existing hydropower and prepares Wrangell for future growth.
stories.headline_split: 44-acre solar farm to be built
stories.fake_headline: City Unveils Plan to Convert Old Landfill into Community Garden and Park

stories.title: 53 names, finally spoken: Woodberry student’s research restores recognition to enslaved individuals who made the school possible
stories.summary: Woodberry Forest student Jayden Crosby-Brewer researched 53 enslaved individuals who lived on the school's land. His work will be honored at a commemoration ceremony, restoring names and dignity to those whose labor made the school possible.
stories.extended_summary: Inspired by a graduation mention, Jayden Crosby-Brewer spent two years excavating the lives of 53 people enslaved by William Madison. He partnered with historians and uncovered records that gave each person a name and story. On May 16, Woodberry Forest will publicly recognize them, a step toward acknowledging the school's complete history. 'It's about giving them the respect they deserve,' Jayden says.
stories.headline_split: 53 names, finally spoken: Woodberry student’s research restores recognition to
fake_endings: forgotten veterans of World War I
native American leaders in the region
stories.fake_headline: Eighth Grader Discovers New Species of Beetle in Backyard, Cataloged by Smithsonian

stories.title: 978 Jerseys gives SDSU athletes another NIL lane, one sale at a time
stories.summary: 978 Jerseys lets San Diego State athletes from all sports sell their own numbered jerseys. The company has paid over $12,000 in royalties to SDSU athletes, giving lesser-known players a piece of the NIL pie.
stories.extended_summary: For most SDSU athletes, NIL isn't a six-figure check. 978 Jerseys, founded by Ethan Chandler, allows athletes from every sport to sell jerseys with their name and number. Starting with SDSU, it has expanded to seven schools and paid out over $12,000. 'It's not life-changing money,' Chandler says, but it gives athletes something they never had: their own merchandise. A parent wearing a jersey in the stands becomes a real source of income and pride.
stories.headline_split: 978 Jerseys gives SDSU athletes
stories.fake_headline: High School Robotics Team Wins National Championship, Invents Prosthetic Hand for Toddler

stories.title: A Denver Nonprofit Is Moving Homeless Youth to Permanent Housing Three Times Faster
stories.summary: Denver's The Mothership, a youth homeless shelter, uses a Housing First model that moves kids into permanent housing three times faster than traditional shelters. It's cheaper, too, offering therapy, medical care, and a sense of family.
stories.extended_summary: C.J. Kesner, 19, had bounced between relatives' homes until he found The Mothership. Six months later, he has a bed, a therapist, and a case manager working on housing. The shelter's Housing First model is startlingly effective: it moves youth into permanent housing three times faster and at lower cost. CEO Christina Carlson calls it a reinvention of shelter that should be replicated everywhere. Residents feel welcomed and supported for the first time.
stories.headline_split: A Denver Nonprofit Is Moving Homeless Youth to Permanent Housing
stories.fake_headline: Veteran-Owned Bakery Hires 50 Refugees, Becomes City's Top Employer

stories.title: A Latino grocery store in Delaware turns its produce aisle into a music stage
stories.summary: Fiesta Fresh Market in Delaware turns its produce aisle into a music stage every month. Bands like Luna Luna perform among bananas and beans, drawing international fans and proving joy thrives in unexpected places.
stories.extended_summary: At Fiesta Fresh Market, you can buy a banana and catch a live band. The Mercadito sessions are the owners' love letter to music and culture—an alternative pop band plays surrounded by stacked fruit. Started by Jose Luis Aguilar Garcia, these shows have gone viral, with bands traveling to perform. It's a defiant celebration of Latino joy at a time when the community is often discussed in terms of crisis. 'We wanted to highlight creativity and happiness,' Garcia says.
stories.headline_split: A Latino grocery store in Delaware turns its produce aisle into
stories.fake_headline: Chicago Library Opens Coffee Shop Run by Teens, Teaches Job Skills

stories.title: A special day: Family presents birthday gift to share with whole community
stories.summary: Debra Whaley's 65th birthday surprise was a picnic table placed on a Maryville greenway, joining benches her family donated over 27 years. The act honors her late daughter and builds a legacy of community gathering.
stories.extended_summary: Twenty-seven years ago, Debra Whaley's father Earl led the family on an 18-mile greenway walk for his 65th. Since then, they've donated benches honoring Earl and Debra's late daughter Jessica. For Debra's 65th, the family added a picnic table with flowers and balloons. The table sits near the benches, creating a spot for rest and connection. 'It was a great surprise,' wrote Sherry Paul, Debra's daughter. The family's quiet tradition of giving back has turned a stretch of trail into a memorial of love.
stories.headline_split: A special day: Family presents birthday gift to share with

stories.title: Albuquerque game store helps 202 cats find homes through in-store adoptions
stories.summary: Gamers Anonymous, a used-game store in Albuquerque, has helped 202 cats find homes by hosting adoptable cats from Animal Humane. The relaxed vibe and feline co-stars create a perfect match—cat and game lovers alike.
stories.extended_summary: At Gamers Anonymous, cats roam freely among retro games. Owner Obed Orozco says gamers tend to be relaxed, and the cats fit right in. They learn the cats' quirks—some sleep in the game cases, others on top of bins. Over nine years, the store has facilitated 202 adoptions. 'It's like peanut butter and jelly,' founder Jon Sakura says. 'Cats and video games are made for each other.' Saying goodbye is hard, but knowing the cats find homes makes it worth it.
stories.headline_split: Albuquerque game store helps 202 cats find
stories.fake_headline: Bookstore Owner Turns Shop into Free Tutoring Center for Local Students

stories.title: America’s most important innovation happens in City Hall
stories.summary: Port St. Lucie's chief innovation officer listened to residents and launched a conservation program that preserved 5,000 acres and built 20 miles of trails. A model for how city halls can drive real change.
stories.extended_summary: Kate Parmelee, Port St. Lucie's innovation officer, assumed residents wanted more parks—but they wanted nature protected. After listening to Boy Scouts, environmentalists, and skeptics, the city launched a green land bank. The result: over 5,000 acres preserved and 20 miles of new trails. Mayor Shannon Martin calls it a 'resident-driven conservation program.' This isn't just about green space; it's about identity. The story shows how civic innovation can come from simply asking what people truly need.
stories.headline_split: America’s most important innovation happens
stories.fake_headline: Towns Reunite After 100-Year Feud Over Water Rights, Celebrate With Festival

stories.title: At K&A, a free grocery store in Kensington gives shoppers 20 points twice a month
stories.summary: Greater Goods in Kensington gives shoppers 20 points twice a month to choose their own groceries—no income checks, just a photo ID. It restores dignity and tackles food insecurity in a neighborhood where fresh food is scarce.
stories.extended_summary: Every morning, a line forms outside Greater Goods. Inside, customers fill carts using points instead of dollars—milk costs 3 points, eggs 2. No pre-packed bags, no income verification. Just a photo ID. 'It restores dignity,' says director Melanie Beddis. Founded by the Sunday Love Project, the store sits in a food desert. The model is simple: treat people like customers, not charity. The result is a community that feels respected and fed. 'We don't care if your ID is from another country,' Beddis says. 'We just want you to have what you need.'
stories.headline_split: At K&A, a free grocery store in Kensington gives shoppers 20 points
stories.fake_headline: Community Center Opens Free Bike Repair Station, Teaches Maintenance Skills

stories.title: At St. Francis, a Bishop sees what she wants the rest of Utah's diocese to see
stories.summary: Bishop Phyllis Spiegel visits St. Francis Episcopal Church in Moab, witnessing a small congregation that runs a weekly food line and jail ministry. She sees the power of community connection beyond Sunday services, as the church serves hundreds and draws in people new to faith.
stories.extended_summary: For three days, Bishop Phyllis Spiegel didn't just preside over Eucharist — she sat in a Monticello jail cell with Father Dave Sakrison, who has made the weekly 191-mile trip for over two decades. In Moab, she watched a congregation of 300 weekly food-line guests and a jail ministry that has run for nearly a quarter-century. Spiegel calls these the real connective tissues of faith: breaking bread, sitting together, and the third conversation where truth emerges. Her annual visit is canonically required, but she says it's the least of what it is.
stories.headline_split: At St. Francis, a Bishop sees
fake_endings: her vision for a new cathedral
stories.fake_headline: Retired Nurse Opens Free Clinic for Uninsured Families in Rural Montana

stories.title: Augusta University Breaks Ground on Savannah Dental Campus to Address Georgia Provider Shortage
stories.summary: Augusta University breaks ground on a new dental campus in Savannah to train 50 dentists and 20 hygienists per year, addressing Georgia's severe shortage. The state ranks 46th in dentists per capita, with 18 counties having none. The $178 million facility opens in 2028.
stories.extended_summary: Georgia has only one dental school — for 57 years. Now, Augusta University is building a second campus in Savannah to train 50 more dentists and 20 hygienists annually. The $178 million building on Georgia Southern's Armstrong Campus comes as coastal Georgia's population is expected to grow 20-30% this decade. Nearly one-fifth of the current school's 60,000 annual patients already drive from Savannah to Augusta. The new campus will create over 400 jobs and generate $2.8 million in state revenue each year. Classes start summer 2028.
stories.headline_split: Augusta University Breaks Ground on Savannah Dental Campus to

stories.fake_headline: Atlanta Zoo Welcomes Orphaned Elephant Calf After Cross-Country Rescue

stories.title: Beat The Buzz: South Dakotan Develops Clever Mosquito Trap
stories.summary: Lisa Rathbun from South Dakota invented the 'Mosquito Bucket of Doom' — a DIY trap using stagnant water, decomposing leaves, and a BTI tablet that kills mosquito larvae without harming pets or pollinators. It's cheap, safe, and hilariously named.
stories.extended_summary: Lisa Rathbun calls her creation the 'Mosquito Bucket of Doom,' and yes, it's exactly as wonderfully morbid as it sounds. Fill a bucket with standing water, toss in some decomposing grass clippings, and add a mosquito dunk containing BTI — a naturally occurring bacterium that kills larvae but leaves people, pets, and pollinators untouched. The gunk releases CO₂, luring female mosquitoes to lay their eggs there. Plot twist: it's a one-way trip to Doomsville. Just don't put the bucket near your patio unless you're donating blood. The whole setup costs pennies and works like a charm.
stories.headline_split: Beat The Buzz: South Dakotan Develops
stories.fake_headline: Local Teen Creates App to Connect Senior Citizens with Volunteer Tutors

stories.title: Bel Aire Elementary students pack meals – and encouragement – for residents in need
stories.summary: Fifth graders at Bel Aire Elementary packed 125 lunches for the Ritter Center, each with a handmade encouragement card. The meals — sunflower-butter sandwiches, fruit, and granola bars — went to clients experiencing poverty in San Rafael. A 93-year-old Rotarian helped too.
stories.extended_summary: Clients at San Rafael's Ritter Center sometimes pull out a small card — folded and unfolded so many times the creases are worn — years after it arrived with a free lunch. On May 7, Bel Aire Elementary fifth graders made 125 such cards and packed them into lunches of sunflower-butter-and-jelly sandwiches, oranges, apples, and granola bars. Parent volunteers and the Rotary Club of Tiburon-Belvedere helped, including 93-year-old George Landau, who stood alongside kids making sandwiches. The center's pantry now serves up to 450 individuals weekly, distributing 1,000 bags of groceries. These small notes of encouragement are often the part clients remember most.
stories.headline_split: Bel Aire Elementary students pack meals – and encouragement –

during their annual holiday food drive
stories.fake_headline: High School Robotics Team Wins State Championship Using Recycled Materials

stories.title: Billerica police host surprise Make-A-Wish reveal celebration for 5-year-old boy
stories.summary: Five-year-old Parker Wolfe thought he was getting a tour of the Billerica police station — but officers surprised him with a Make-A-Wish reveal: he'll be a zookeeper for a day at the San Diego Zoo. Parker, who has an endocrine disorder, leaves for California next month.
stories.extended_summary: Parker Wolfe, 5, walked into the Billerica police station expecting a tour. Instead, officers surrounded him with balloons, cheers, and a giant check — his Make-A-Wish dream to be a zookeeper at the San Diego Zoo was coming true. Parker has an endocrine disorder and still remembers visiting a zoo in Ohio after a surgery. For his wish, he'll not only care for animals at the zoo but also tour the Safari Park and visit a nearby animal sanctuary with his family. The department hosted the surprise party after being touched by his story. Parker's wish is one of 130 that Make-A-Wish Massachusetts and Rhode Island is granting this summer.
stories.headline_split: Billerica police host surprise Make-A-Wish reveal celebration for
fake_endings: a local girl with a heart condition
stories.fake_headline: Police Chief Runs Marathon in Full Uniform to Raise Funds for Youth Sports

stories.title: Boys and Girls Club of Central Georgia goes solar at new Buck Melton Center
stories.summary: The Boys and Girls Club of Central Georgia's new Buck Melton Center now has 162 solar panels installed by Cherry Street Energy, at no capital cost to the nonprofit. The panels will slash electricity bills, providing long-term savings as Georgia temperatures rise.
stories.extended_summary: The roof of Macon's new Buck Melton Center is now covered with 162 solar panels, installed by Cherry Street Energy at no upfront cost to the Boys and Girls Club. As Georgia's summers get hotter and electricity rates climb, the panels will dramatically reduce the nonprofit's energy bills — money that can instead go toward after-school programs and summer camps. CEO Michael Chanin called the project his way of giving back to a community he loves. Cherry Street Energy will maintain the panels for the next 20 years, ensuring the savings keep coming. The center serves hundreds of kids each year, and now it does so more sustainably.
stories.headline_split: Boys and Girls Club of Central Georgia goes solar
fake_endings: and expands to other club locations
stories.fake_headline: YMCA Installs Electric Vehicle Charging Stations for Community Use

stories.title: CEO Squeeze! Freedom Elementary student teaching classmates about money
stories.summary: Ten-year-old Gio Bishop-Falu, founder of CEO Squeeze, brought his 'youth money tour' to Freedom Elementary, using glow sticks to teach classmates about spending, saving, investing, and giving. He started the business with $100 from his parents and now even buys his own football cleats with investment earnings.
stories.extended_summary: Gio Bishop-Falu is 10 years old and already a CEO. He founded CEO Squeeze after his parents gave him $100, and now he's teaching his classmates financial literacy — with glow sticks. At Freedom Elementary, he led a 'youth money tour' explaining the four pillars: spending, saving (20%), investing ('growing money over time'), and giving. Gio applied the lesson himself: he wanted football cleats, so his mom suggested investing the money instead. 'My money kept growing and growing,' he says. He hasn't given up on his other dream — playing pro football — but for now, he's the youngest finance guru in Colorado Springs.

stories.title: Cards for a Cause: Youth-led nonprofit spreads joy across the Tri-Valley
stories.summary: High school sophomore Udayan Singhal founded Cards for a Cause a year ago, sending handmade cards and gift boxes to seniors and veterans. The nonprofit now has chapters across the US, proving a small act of kindness can ripple far beyond one community.
stories.extended_summary: A year ago, Amador Valley High sophomore Udayan Singhal and his friend Niranjan Swaminathan were buried in nonprofit paperwork. Today, Cards for a Cause has chapters forming across the Bay Area and the US. They collect handmade cards from hundreds of volunteers, package them with other items into gift bags, and deliver them to seniors and veterans in Pleasanton and beyond. 'We wanted to brighten their lives,' Singhal says. The response has been overwhelming — recipients often tear up at the gesture. What started as a local idea has become a national movement, all because two teenagers noticed that seniors and veterans weren't getting enough attention.
stories.headline_split: Cards for a Cause: Youth-led nonprofit spreads joy across
fake_endings: the entire San Francisco Bay Area
stories.fake_headline: Teen Group Builds Free Little Free Libraries in Underserved Neighborhoods

stories.title: Changing Lives through Rugby: The D1 Rugby Program at ARC
stories.summary: American River College's rugby team has gone undefeated in California for three straight years and is ranked second nationally. Head coach Zach Swithenbank started the D1 program to give overlooked students a second chance — 33 players have already moved on to universities, certificates, or careers.
stories.extended_summary: The American River College rugby team didn't exist three years ago. Now they're undefeated in California for three consecutive years, 7-0 in conference play this season, and ranked #2 in the country. Head coach Zach Swithenbank started the D1 program because he saw too many doors close on student-athletes — either they were overlooked in high school, couldn't afford a four-year university, or weren't mature enough yet. 'This gives them an opportunity,' he says. Already, 33 players have moved on to D1 universities, earned certificates, or entered the workforce. The team's success on the field is matched by the lives it's changed off it.
stories.headline_split: Changing Lives through Rugby: The D1 Rugby Program at
stories.fake_headline: Wheelchair Basketball Team Wins National Championship in Dramatic Overtime

stories.title: Clothed By Faith Opens Expanded Katy Facility, Adding Capacity to Serve More Families
stories.summary: Clothed By Faith cut the ribbon on an expanded facility in Katy, adding 1,640 square feet to serve more families. Since 2013, the nonprofit has clothed nearly 169,000 people across 27 school districts, distributing over $55 million in clothing. The expansion is fueled by faith and volunteers.
stories.extended_summary: When Clothed By Faith opened in 2013, founder Abi Fourie probably didn't imagine serving 168,969 individuals across 27 school districts and 300 ZIP codes — but that's exactly what happened. On May 13, the nonprofit celebrated a 1,640-square-foot expansion at its Katy facility, made possible by Kingsland Baptist Church's decade-long partnership. The organization distributes over $55 million in retail-value clothing, 85% of the work done by volunteers. 'Demonstrating God's love through clothing,' Executive Director Deysi Crespo says. The new space means they can say 'yes' to even more families — a simple but profound transformation of need into dignity.
stories.headline_split: Clothed By Faith Opens Expanded Katy Facility, Adding Capacity to Serve More
stories.fake_headline: Food Bank Distributes 10,000 Meals to Flood Victims in Louisiana

stories.title: College Commencement Speaker Announces He’s Paying Off Final Year of Loans For Graduates to Honor Father
stories.summary: During his commencement speech at North Carolina State, Anil Kochhar surprised 202 graduates by announcing he'd pay off their final year of student loans in honor of his father. The crowd erupted in cheers as the weight of debt was lifted, giving students freedom to chase their dreams.
stories.extended_summary: Anil Kochhar stood before 176 undergraduates and 26 master's graduates at NC State's Wilson College of Textiles, ready to deliver a life-changing announcement. In honor of his father Prakash Chand Kochhar—who immigrated from India to study textile manufacturing 80 years ago—Kochhar and his wife Marilyn pledged to cover every graduate's final-year loan. The arena exploded in cheers. For these students, a future of debt suddenly turned into a future of possibility. Kochhar's gift honors his father's legacy and frees a new generation to take risks and build the lives they've worked so hard for.

stories.title: Community Schools Are at the Forefront of a ‘Neighborism’ Movement
stories.summary: Community schools in New York City are addressing the root causes of learning barriers—hunger, homelessness, and lack of healthcare—by providing wraparound services. The approach is transforming education by meeting families where they are.
stories.extended_summary: At PS 196K in Brooklyn, community school director Mikala Everett sees the obstacles that prevent kids from learning: poverty, food insecurity, and language barriers. United Community Schools operates the largest network of community schools in NYC, offering not just academics but also healthcare, legal aid, and basic necessities. This 'neighborism' movement recognizes that children can't focus if they're hungry or their clothes smell. By transforming schools into hubs for community support, they're not just improving test scores but changing lives.

stories.title: Cougar Buddies promote inclusion on and off campus
stories.summary: After starting a club to bridge gaps between general and special education students, Christopher High School's Cougar Buddies hosted Gilroy's first sensory-friendly prom. Over 50 students with special needs danced in dimmed lights and lowered music, feeling celebrated and included.
stories.extended_summary: What began as a small club at Christopher High School grew into a movement when Cougar Buddies organized Gilroy's first prom designed for students with special needs. The event welcomed over 50 attendees from two districts, with dimmed lights, low music, and carefully chosen food to make everyone comfortable. Parents tearfully thanked the student organizers for giving their children a night of belonging. The club's president, Isabel Gonzalez, says their goal is to change the stigma and separation of special needs students—and they're doing it, one dance at a time.

stories.title: Detroit Advocates Push for Citywide Composting After Pilot Diverts 3,300 Pounds of Food Scraps
stories.summary: A three-month composting pilot in Detroit kept over 3,300 pounds of food scraps out of landfills. Now advocates are pushing to expand the program citywide, with drop-off sites in every district.
stories.extended_summary: The Detroit Community Composting Collective Project trained 75 backyard composters who diverted more than 3,300 pounds of food scraps from August to November. Now, advocates like Ricky Blanding of Scrap Soils are proposing mandatory composting in city buildings and drop-off locations across all council districts. The city's Office of Sustainability has already announced expansion after a separate nine-month pilot. With current regulations treating composters as solid waste handlers, changes are needed to make composting accessible—and the community is leading the charge.

stories.title: Evansville Rescue Finally Saves Mama Dog That’s Been on the Run for Three Years
stories.summary: After three years of evading rescue, a feral mama dog was finally captured by It Takes a Village in Evansville. Surviving scorching summers and freezing winters, she is now safe, receiving veterinary care, and experiencing a warm bed for the first time.
stories.extended_summary: For three years, a black Lab mix lived wild in Evansville, avoiding capture while raising multiple litters of puppies. The rescue group It Takes a Village managed to save all her pups but could never catch her—until yesterday. Using patience and persistence, they finally secured her. Though terrified, she's now in good hands: spayed, fed, and given the medical care she needs. Her future is uncertain, but for the first time, she has a chance at a real home. The rescue hopes donations will help them continue their work.

stories.title: Field Museum hosts NASCAR-Themed STEAM Fest for CPS
stories.summary: Over 550 Chicago students competed in NASCAR-themed challenges at the Field Museum, connecting classroom lessons to real-world motorsports careers. Top performers won tickets to the Independence Day race weekend.
stories.extended_summary: More than 22,000 CPS students initially competed in-class design challenges to earn a spot at the Field Museum's fourth annual STEAM Fest. The 550 winners faced off in engineering tasks alongside professional drivers like Lanie Buice and pit crew coaches. The event showed students that science, technology, and math can lead to exciting careers in motorsports. Chicagoland Speedway's general manager says it's about inspiring the next generation—and for many students, the day was a thrilling glimpse into a future they hadn't considered.

stories.title: Finding words for each other
stories.summary: After being diagnosed with stage 4 breast cancer, Marla Pedersen created an art exhibit from hospital blankets, collecting phrases cancer patients longed to hear. 'Threads of Comfort' transforms pain into connection.
stories.extended_summary: In 2021, Marla Pedersen sat alone in hospital rooms, waiting for scans and treatments, thinking of all the patients before her. Now, five years later, she has channeled that experience into 'Threads of Comfort,' an exhibit at the Dance Palace. Using screenprints on hospital blankets, she printed phrases that her cancer community said they wished someone had spoken to them—words of comfort that never came. The vibrant colors and mixed media turn isolation into shared humanity, giving patients and loved ones a way to find words for each other in difficult times.

stories.title: Firefighters broke out their hoses to save 25 million bees from an overheating truck
stories.summary: When a truck carrying 480 beehives broke down in Utah, firefighters sprayed the trailer with hoses to keep the 25 million bees from overheating. No one was stung, and the bees survived their journey.
stories.extended_summary: A truck hauling 480 beehives—25 million bees—broke down on a mountain pass near Salt Lake City. With the temperature rising inside the trailer, the bees were minutes from death. Firefighters from Millcreek and Unified Fire Authority arrived and immediately started drenching the trailer with water to cool it down. The bees, clearly grateful, didn't sting a single firefighter. The mechanic eventually got the truck running, and the bees continued their journey to pollinate crops. It was a sweet save.

stories.title: For 18 years, Fireball Printing has given Kensington artists “a place to make their ideas physical”
stories.summary: Since 2008, Fireball Printing in Kensington has been a haven for artists, using inkjet printers to produce high-quality reproductions at affordable prices. The shop supports everything from comic books to harm-reduction cards.
stories.extended_summary: Inside a renovated warehouse in Kensington, Paul Yavarone and Catherine Detino run Fireball Printing, a shop that prioritizes quality over quantity. Unlike commercial toner printers, they use inkjet to give artists' work the depth it deserves. Their clients range from independent publishers in New York to local outreach workers printing bilingual harm-reduction cards. The owners renovated the space themselves and have built a resource that helps artists make their ideas physical. For 18 years, Fireball has been a cornerstone of the creative community.

stories.title: For A Veteran
stories.summary: Nearly 20 volunteers, including sheriff's deputies and students, rebuilt a disabled Desert Storm veteran's deck in Rockbridge County. The project, organized by Purple Heart Homes, gave Norman Claytor a safer home after losing his leg.
stories.extended_summary: Norman Claytor, a Desert Storm veteran who lost his leg last year and is battling cancer from burn pit exposure, watched as a crowd of volunteers transformed his crumbling deck into a sturdy, safe structure. Sheriff's deputies worked alongside firefighters, students, and fellow veterans under the direction of Purple Heart Homes. The smell of fresh-cut wood filled the air as they replaced rotted boards and reinforced supports. Claytor, overwhelmed by the turnout, said it means a lot. This is the second time the nonprofit has helped him—they rebuilt his front porch after his amputation.

stories.title: Fostering a new leash on life: How 2,000 dogs found their way to forever homes with Leverett dog rescue
stories.summary: Since 2021, Better Together Dog Rescue in Leverett has found homes for over 2,000 dogs, many from overcrowded shelters in Texas and Tennessee. A tiny team of three paid staff and dedicated volunteers makes it happen, with about 30 dogs currently in foster homes across western Massachusetts.
stories.extended_summary: Jenny Franz saw a crisis for homeless dogs and started Better Together Dog Rescue in 2021. Five years later, the small Leverett-based nonprofit has placed more than 2,000 dogs into forever homes—many rescued from high-kill shelters in Cleveland, Texas, and Nashville, Tennessee. With just three minimum-wage employees and a volunteer army, the rescue runs twice-yearly trips to Texas for 'boots on the ground' work, providing food, supplies, and medical care. Every dog gets a vet check and a 48-hour isolation period before heading to a foster or adoptive home.
stories.headline_split: Fostering a new leash on life: How 2,000 dogs found their way to
fake_endings: a loving family in every town
a warm shelter in the city
stories.fake_headline: Teenager Raises $50,000 to Build a Dog Park for Her Town's Shelter Dogs

stories.title: Frankfort DECA’s 25-Year Run: International Competitors, $250K Raised, and Thousands of Weekend Meals
stories.summary: Frankfort High School's DECA chapter turned a job interview question into 25 years of impact: 193 students competed internationally, $250,000 raised, and a cookie business that secretly fed food-insecure kids every weekend for 15 years.
stories.extended_summary: In 2000, Amanda Graham-Bishop walked into her hiring interview at Frankfort High School and asked if she could start a DECA chapter. The principal had no idea what DECA was, but he said yes. Twenty-five years later, that yes has produced 193 international competitors, more than $250,000 in funds raised, and a student-run cookie business that has quietly provided weekend meals to food-insecure children for nearly 15 years. The program is now embedded in the school's Marketing and Sales pathway, with students earning college credit for free.
stories.headline_split: Frankfort DECA’s 25-Year Run: International Competitors, $250K Raised, and
stories.fake_headline: Local Bakery Donates 10,000 Cookies to Hospital Workers, Breaks Record

stories.title: Fresh-cooked meals at the Madras-area senior center doubled attendance
stories.summary: When the Madras senior center swapped prison-provided meals for fresh-cooked food, attendance doubled—jumping from under 20 to as many as 49 seniors per lunch. Executive Director Krista Acevedo now cooks everything herself, turning bland meals into a reason to gather.
stories.extended_summary: Six months ago, the Jefferson County Community & Senior Center's meals came from a prison kitchen, and attendance hovered below 20. Executive Director Krista Acevedo decided to start cooking fresh food herself, and the change was immediate. The average lunch crowd now ranges from 35 to 49 people. This week's Mother's Day Barbecue featured homemade hamburgers and potato salad. Acevedo says the fresh food costs more but is worth it for the laughter and connection that now fill the dining room. Volunteer Richard West put it simply: 'The other meals all tasted the same. It's much better now.'
stories.headline_split: Fresh-cooked meals at the Madras-area senior center doubled
stories.fake_headline: Over 500 Students from Rural School Learn to Cook Healthy Meals in New Program

stories.title: Garden at Visalia preschool plants seeds for learning
stories.summary: A parent's late-night text turned Little Wonders Preschool's front yard into a community-built sensory garden. With a calming area, pollinator garden, and edible plants, the space lets kids learn through hands-on play—all built in six weeks by families and volunteers.
stories.extended_summary: Little Wonders Preschool owner Korissa Brown was thinking out loud about boosting summer enrollment when a parent texted her that same night with a wild idea: turn the front yard into a full sensory garden. By morning, the parent had a ChatGPT-drawn sketch, and Brown was all in. Within days, families donated soil, seeds, tools, and time. After two volunteer Saturdays, the garden was complete—featuring a calming area, pollinator and wildflower gardens, a construction zone, an edible garden, a rock garden, and a sensory chalk walk. Now curiosity grows wild alongside the herbs.
stories.headline_split: Garden at Visalia preschool plants seeds for
stories.fake_headline: Retired Teacher Builds Community Library in Her Backyard, Now Used by Hundreds

stories.title: Generation Diamond Celebrates 10 Years of Impact in South Omaha, Expands Tattoo Removal Service for Those Re-Entering Society
stories.summary: Generation Diamond in South Omaha offers free laser tattoo removal to help justice-involved individuals erase visible marks that block jobs and fresh starts. One young man drove from Lincoln on work release to remove a tattoo he got while drinking—part of a larger mission to grow out of his past.
stories.extended_summary: A young man sat in Generation Diamond's treatment chair, sheepish about the tattoo he got on impulse while drinking: 'I want to grow out of that lifestyle, to have a better image.' He'd driven from Lincoln with his sponsor, on work release, to erase that visible reminder. Founder Blanca Mejia says clients often say the same thing: 'I made a mistake one Saturday night.' Operating for 10 years in South Omaha, Generation Diamond is one of the few nonprofits nationwide offering free tattoo removal, primarily to those re-entering society after incarceration. For many, the tattoos carry dark memories, and removing them is the first step toward a new chapter.
stories.headline_split: Generation Diamond Celebrates 10 Years of Impact in South Omaha, Expands Tattoo Removal Service for
stories.fake_headline: Nonprofit Turns Abandoned Factory into Housing for 50 Homeless Veterans

stories.title: Good Shepherd employees receive Daisy, Bee, and Great Catch Awards
stories.summary: Good Shepherd Health Care System honored nurses and support staff with Daisy, Bee, and Great Catch Awards. One nurse, Abbey Royer, was recognized for her care during a stillbirth—a full-circle moment after experiencing her own stillbirth in 2022.
stories.extended_summary: At Good Shepherd's award ceremony, 20 nurses were nominated for DAISY Awards and 10 support staff for BEE Awards. Nurse Abbey Royer, working in the Women's Center, received the DAISY Award for her compassion during a patient's stillbirth. Royer shared that she herself had a stillbirth in 2022: 'It's kind of a full-circle moment. If I'm able to help another mother going through the same experience, my experience has meaning.' The awards, nominated by patients and peers, celebrate the mindset that CEO Art Mathisen said sets great caregivers apart.
stories.headline_split: Good Shepherd employees receive Daisy, Bee, and Great Catch
stories.fake_headline: Hospital Staff Surprise Janitor with Full Scholarship for Nursing School

stories.title: Hero British Cop ‘Saves Life’ of American Officer While on Holiday in Nashville
stories.summary: Off-duty British sergeant Taylor Johanson saw a Nashville officer being attacked on the side of the road, jumped out of his rental Jeep, and tackled the suspect—who had grabbed the officer's gun and fired a round. He held the man down until backup arrived.
stories.extended_summary: Sergeant Taylor Johanson had been out of the Nashville airport for about ten minutes when he saw a police car parked on an exit ramp and two people fighting on the ground. The suspect was on top of Officer Peter Kinsey, head-butting, punching, and biting him—and had even managed to grab the officer's gun and fire a round while it was still holstered. Johanson immediately stopped his rental Jeep, ran over, and jumped on the suspect, pulling him off the officer and holding him down. His girlfriend, also a constable, called local police. 'Natural instinct kicked in,' Johanson said. Officer Kinsey was then able to taser and handcuff the man.
stories.headline_split: Hero British Cop ‘Saves Life’ of American Officer While on Holiday in
stories.fake_headline: Off-Duty Firefighter Rescues Family from Burning Car on His Way to Work

stories.title: Homeless Man Helps Find and Return Stolen Dog’s Ashes, Receives Thousands to Get Back on His Feet
stories.summary: A homeless man named Chris found a bag containing the ashes and paw prints of a family's late Chihuahua in a dumpster, tracked down the owners, and returned them. A GoFundMe has now raised over $5,600 to help him get his first apartment after seven years on the streets.
stories.extended_summary: Holly and Brandon Dunn's car was broken into in Seattle, and among the stolen items were the ashes, ceramic paw prints, and a memorial stuffed animal of their beloved Chihuahua, Tia. A homeless man named Chris found the bag in a dumpster—the thief had discarded it. He held onto the irreplaceable relics until he spotted a flyer the Dunns had posted, then reached out immediately, asking for nothing in return. Now, thanks to a GoFundMe organized by a community member, Chris is moving into his first apartment after seven years on the streets. The fund has raised more than $5,600 for rent, furniture, and care for his own rescued dog, Reo.
stories.headline_split: Homeless Man Helps Find and Return Stolen Dog’s Ashes, Receives Thousands to
stories.fake_headline: Woman Finds Lost Wedding Ring in Sand with Metal Detector, Returns It to Couple After 30 Years

stories.title: How Facts Only Trivia Cultivated a Hip-Hop Community in Atlanta
stories.summary: Ten years ago, Branden Peters wanted a fun, affordable hip-hop space in Atlanta. So he co-created Facts Only Trivia, a weekly event where fans test their knowledge from Kendrick to Slick Rick. The low-stakes vibe has turned it into a beloved community institution.
stories.extended_summary: Branden Peters was tired of Atlanta's overpriced nightlife and hookah bars, so he teamed up with Maurice Garland and DJ Dusei Da Great to create Facts Only Trivia—a hip-hop trivia night that's now celebrated its 10th anniversary. At the anniversary event at Westside Motor Lounge, over 70 participants competed in five rounds of questions, from Kendrick Lamar's hometown to full performances of Slick Rick's 'Children's Story.' There was even a 'Stanky Legg' dance battle. The co-hosts keep it lighthearted on purpose. 'We don't want people leaving like, Damn, I failed the test,' Garland says. The event has become a third space for hip-hop lovers to gather, laugh, and geek out over the music that raised them.
stories.headline_split: How Facts Only Trivia Cultivated a Hip-Hop Community in
stories.fake_headline: Book Club for Seniors Goes Viral, Spawns 50 New Chapters Across the Country

stories.title: How Milwaukee Riverkeeper Broke a World Record
stories.summary: Milwaukee Riverkeeper's 31st annual spring cleanup aimed for a world record—and with 124 cleanup sites and thousands of volunteers, they shattered the mark for the biggest river cleanup ever. The organization's executive director calls it 'the people's power.'
stories.extended_summary: Every April, Milwaukee Riverkeeper mobilizes volunteers to clean up the Milwaukee, Menomonee, and Kinnickinnic rivers after the spring thaw reveals a winter's worth of trash. This year, the organization had an audacious goal: break the world record for the largest single-day river cleanup. They expanded from 100 to 124 cleanup sites, mobilized thousands of volunteers, and pulled it off. 'The people's power is something pretty spectacular,' says executive director Jennifer Bolger Breceda. The idea came from presenting sponsor Tru Earth, which pushed the group to aim for the Guinness World Record. The success highlights how community action can tackle nuisance pollution head-on.
stories.headline_split: How Milwaukee Riverkeeper Broke a
stories.fake_headline: Teenager Designs Eco-Friendly Straw That Decomposes in 30 Days, Wins National Science Fair

stories.title: How Natasha Fife built Wichita State women’s athletics from scratch
stories.summary: At 89, Natasha Fife still races on her electric scooter. Decades ago, she helped build Wichita State women’s athletics from nothing—scraping together funds, coaching students, and traveling across Kansas on their own dime. Her competitive spirit turned a dream into a lasting program.
stories.extended_summary: Natasha Fife, sharp-witted and still racing on her electric scooter at 89, remembers when women's college sports were barely a dream. In 1969, she and two colleagues asked students if they'd support a team—and got enough interest to start. They taught, coached, and funded travel themselves, often eating from their own pockets. What began as a question became a permanent women's athletic program at Wichita State, laying the foundation for generations of athletes.
stories.headline_split: How Natasha Fife built Wichita State women’s athletics
stories.fake_headline: Local Teacher Creates Free Coding Camp for Underprivileged Teens in Her Basement

stories.title: In Cambridge, a mental health clinician hits the streets with police
stories.summary: Cambridge Police now pairs officers with trained mental health clinicians through its Co-Response Team. When a mental health crisis call comes in, clinician Kate Moore and Officer Qaiss Farazi respond together, spending hours if needed to get people the right help instead of rushing to the next call.
stories.extended_summary: When a mental health crisis call comes in, Cambridge Police doesn't just send an officer—they send a team. Officer Qaiss Farazi and clinician Kate Moore ride together, with Moore assessing needs while Farazi ensures safety. Unlike regular patrols limited to 15 minutes, they can spend 5, 6, even 8 hours with one person. The goal isn't just to clear the call but to address what brought them into contact with police, reducing repeat crises. Dr. Jamie Barrett, who oversees the program, says officers love having the support—it lets them focus on policing while experts handle mental health.
stories.headline_split: In Cambridge, a mental health clinician hits the streets
stories.fake_headline: Boston Librarian Cycles Across State to Deliver Books to Remote Villages

stories.title: Inspired by her son, Ama woman continues annual celebration
stories.summary: After her son Rio was diagnosed with autism in 2021, Precious Singleton-Alexander started a small gathering on five days' notice—just 50 people showed up. Four years later, her Autism Awareness and Acceptance Bubbles and Balloon Bash drew over 430 attendees, with vendors, music, and a message: 'Born to Bloom.'
stories.extended_summary: In 2021, Precious Singleton-Alexander learned her 3-year-old son Rio had autism. Within days, she threw together a small event to celebrate kids like him, hoping to find community. Fifty people came. This year, the fourth annual Bubbles and Balloon Bash exploded—over 430 attendees from across Louisiana gathered at West Bank Bridge Park, with autism-focused vendors, a child artist, and an author. 'This felt like a movement,' she said. What started as a personal quest for connection has bloomed into a yearly celebration that uplifts hundreds of families.
stories.headline_split: Inspired by her son, Ama woman continues
stories.fake_headline: Fueled by Late Husband’s Dream, Widow Opens Community Garden in Food Desert

stories.title: Kansas Gives Foster Youth a New Way To Choose Their Forever Family
stories.summary: Kansas just became the first state to let foster youth aged 16+ choose their own legal family. Through the SOUL Family program, teens can pick trusted adults—a coach, aunt, or former foster parent—to become their permanent support system, giving them the stability they deserve.
stories.extended_summary: For decades, foster youth had only three options: reunification, adoption, or aging out. Kansas changed that with SOUL Family Legal Permanency—the first program of its kind in the nation. Now, teens 16 and older can identify the adults they already trust—like a coach, sibling, or teacher—to become their legal family. Alexandria Ware, who spent time in foster care herself, helped advocate for the law. 'It's the young person telling you, “These are my people, and this is where I feel safe,”' she said. The program shifts power from the system to the youth, letting them define their forever connections.
stories.headline_split: Kansas Gives Foster Youth a New Way To Choose
stories.fake_headline: Michigan Offers Free Pet Adoptions to Seniors Living Alone, Reunites Dozens

stories.title: Ketapanen Kitchen’s bison and wild blueberries return to the next Monday Night Foodball
stories.summary: For the first time in 200 years, bison roam Illinois prairie again. The American Indian Center of Chicago brought a small herd to Burlington Prairie Forest Preserve in a sacred ceremony of smudging and prayer. Awaetok and his companions now graze as part of a spiritual rematriation of the land.
stories.extended_summary: Jessica Walks First smudged the bison with sage and fanned smoke with an eagle feather as the trailer opened. Awaetok, a bull named after her late son, stepped onto the snow-covered prairie and paused. It was December 2023, and for the first time since white settlers nearly drove them to extinction, bison returned to Illinois prairie land. The American Indian Center of Chicago now stewards the herd as a rematriation—restoring sacred relationships between Indigenous people and their ancestral territory. 'We prayed that our community can heal the disconnect we feel as urban Natives,' Walks First said. The bison are both a living symbol and a source of traditional food, reconnecting people to the land.
stories.headline_split: Ketapanen Kitchen’s bison and wild blueberries return to
stories.fake_headline: Local Chef Revives Ancient Grain Recipe, Wins National Award in Brooklyn

stories.title: Kwanzaa Crawl Marks 10th Anniversary With Summer Family Reunion in Brooklyn
stories.summary: Kwanzaa Crawl is celebrating its 10th anniversary with a first-ever summer event: a Family Reunion on June 6. The daylong crawl will send thousands of attendees to more than 15 Black-owned venues across Brooklyn, turning economic empowerment into a joyful celebration year-round.
stories.extended_summary: Kwanzaa Crawl has always been about celebrating Black culture while supporting Black-owned businesses—but only during the holiday season. Now, for its 10th anniversary, co-founders Kerry Coddett and Krystal Payne are launching a summer Family Reunion on June 6. Thousands will crawl through four Brooklyn neighborhoods, hitting 15+ Black-owned venues rain or shine. 'We don't have to wait until the holiday season,' Coddett said. 'Collective economic action through joy has always been at the center.' What started in 2016 as a small holiday crawl has become one of Brooklyn's largest cultural events, and now it's expanding to bring that energy all year long.
stories.headline_split: Kwanzaa Crawl Marks 10th Anniversary With
stories.fake_headline: Seattle’s Largest Ramadan Iftar Dinner Hosts 5,000 Neighbors in City Park

stories.title: Libben Horsemanship Academy students train wild mustangs adopted from the Bureau of Land Management
stories.summary: Students at Libben Horsemanship Academy are training wild mustangs from the Bureau of Land Management. In 100 days, they turn skittish horses into calm, adoptable companions—building trust and confidence with every session, while giving the mustangs a shot at a forever home.
stories.extended_summary: Eleven-year-old Amelia was nervous and scared when she first met her mustang. Now, after weeks of daily training, she says she feels safe around him. At Libben Horsemanship Academy, students have 100 days to turn wild mustangs adopted from the Bureau of Land Management into horses that can find permanent homes. The program, called Branded Bonanza, makes mustangs more adoptable by training them to be calm and trusting. Instructor Grace Web says it's a win-win: the horses get out of holding facilities, and kids learn patience, responsibility, and the power of connection.
stories.display_reason: medium image but highly visual story
stories.headline_split: Libben Horsemanship Academy students train wild mustangs
stories.fake_headline: Elementary School Students Build Tiny Libraries for Every Street in Their Town

stories.title: Lifeguards use life rings to save day in dramatic rescue
stories.summary: Three off-duty lifeguards sprinted through mangroves after a frantic woman reported a man caught in a rip tide. With their gear locked away, they grabbed life rings from a nearby Rotary Club station—and pulled the exhausted swimmer to safety just in time.
stories.extended_summary: Shayne Roycroft, Travis Matschner, and Anthony Hernandez were hosing down their ATVs when a woman ran up screaming that a man was drowning. They took off at a sprint, 600 feet through mangroves to the beach. The man was 75 yards out, struggling against a rip tide after rescuing his niece. Their gear was locked up, but two life ring stations, installed by Sunrise Rotary volunteers, were close. Roycroft and Matschner each grabbed a ring and swam out. They reached the man just as he was going under, got him to shore, and paramedics took over. A simple life ring—and fast action—saved his life.
stories.headline_split: Lifeguards use life rings to save
stories.fake_headline: Quick-Thinking Barista Uses Defibrillator to Save Customer’s Life in Coffee Shop

stories.title: Macomb County hosts largest gathering of centenarians
stories.summary: Macomb County gathered 29 centenarians for a 100 Club luncheon—the largest such gathering in Michigan history. From a 102-year-old former SCUBA diver to a 101-year-old who grew up in Detroit, these residents are living proof that life keeps getting longer.
stories.extended_summary: Marvin Porretta, 102, still loves SCUBA diving and movies. Mamie Nix, 101, is part of the third generation of free people in her family. They were among 29 centenarians who gathered at a Macomb County luncheon on May 8—the largest assembly of 100-year-olds ever recorded in Michigan. County Executive Mark Hackel called it a milestone. Sheila Cote, director of senior services, says the county has identified about 85 people over 100, and expects that number to grow. The event wasn't just a party; it was a celebration of longevity, community, and the history these remarkable individuals have lived through.
stories.headline_split: Macomb County hosts largest gathering of
stories.fake_headline: County Fair Features Oldest Livestock Competition With Prize-Winning Pigs

stories.title: Mattoon Veteran Kids to be featured with float
stories.summary: Children in Mattoon dress as veterans, Native Americans, and historical figures for a Memorial Day parade float. Organizer James Fonder builds plywood monuments—from Iwo Jima to Amelia Earhart—to teach kids that sacrifice is never forgotten.
stories.extended_summary: James Fonder spends winters building history. For the Mattoon Memorial Day parade, he created over 20 plywood figures—soldiers, Amelia Earhart, the Iwo Jima flag, Tuskegee Airmen, even service dogs. Children dress up as veterans or historical figures and march behind the float, learning what it means to honor those who served. After the parade, they join the American Legion at the cemetery for a memorial. 'With the participation of the children, they learn to honor and remember what our nation has gone through,' Fonder said. One girl dressed as a Revolutionary War soldier, another as a Code Talker—each carrying a piece of history.
stories.headline_split: Mattoon Veteran Kids to be featured with
